serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of this dish as a ramen topping or side dish is approximately 100 g, equivalent to a small bowl or condiment-sized portion.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Bamboo shoots, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (FDC ID 169231) and Onions, spring or scallions (includes tops and bulb), raw (FDC ID 170000); composite estimate weighted 80% bamboo shoots, 20% spring onions with soy sauce and sesame oil adjustment for sodium and fat.
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for braised bamboo shoots with spring onions exists in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore Foodo, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked bamboo shoots (FDC ID 169231: ~27 kcal, 2.6 g protein, 0.3 g fat, 5.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g) and scallions (FDC ID 170000: ~32 kcal, 1.8 g protein, 0.2 g fat, 7.3 g carbohydrate per 100 g), adjusted upward for soy sauce sodium (~450 mg/100 g dish), sesame oil fat contribution (~3 g/100 g dish), and mirin sugar contribution (~4 g/100 g dish). The input record values (energy 80 kcal, protein 3.5 g, fat 3.0 g, carbohydrate 9.0 g, sodium 450 mg) are plausible for this preparation and are retained with medium confidence. Cholesterol is confirmed zero as all ingredients are plant-based. Trans fat is confirmed zero. Calcium 15 mg and iron 0.5 mg are consistent with USDA bamboo shoot data.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Analogous estimate: bamboo shoots are a low-starch, high-fibre vegetable; GI estimated at approximately 20 by analogy with other low-starch cooked vegetables (e.g., green beans GI ~15, mixed cooked vegetables GI ~20–25) from the University of Sydney GI Database.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for braised bamboo shoots with spring onions exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 20 is estimated by analogy with low-starch cooked vegetables documented in the International Tables of Glycemic Index (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (20 × 9.0) / 100 = 1.8 per 100 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 450 mg per 100 g, exceeding the 400 mg per 100 g threshold for hypertension-friendly foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce or low-sodium soy sauce when ordering this dish, as the current sodium content of 450 mg per 100 g exceeds recommended limits for blood pressure management; a reduced-sodium preparation could bring this dish within acceptable range.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (9 g per 100 g) and meaningful dietary fibre (3 g per 100 g) supporting moderate glycaemic response; estimated GI of 20
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate load (9 g per 100 g), high fibre content (3 g per 100 g) that slows glucose absorption, and an estimated glycaemic index of approximately 20; however, portion size should be monitored when combined with high-carbohydrate staples such as rice or noodles.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (450 mg per 100 g) is a cardiovascular risk factor; saturated fat is low at 0.5 g per 100 g and cholesterol is zero, which are favourable
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat (0.5 g per 100 g), zero cholesterol, and plant-based ingredients are cardiovascular-friendly, but the sodium content of 450 mg per 100 g is a concern for individuals with cardiovascular disease who should limit sodium intake; requesting a low-sodium preparation is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bamboo Shoots with Spring Onions (タケノコとネギの煮物, Takenoko to Negi no Nimono) is a Japanese-style braised or simmered vegetable dish consisting of tender bamboo shoot pieces and fresh spring onions (scallions) cooked in a seasoning base typically comprising soy sauce, mirin, and sesame oil. The dish is commonly served as a side dish or as a topping for ramen noodle bowls in Japanese cuisine. The texture of the bamboo shoots is firm yet yielding, with a mild, slightly earthy flavour that absorbs the umami-rich braising liquid. Spring onions contribute a fresh, mildly pungent note and a soft texture when cooked. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 80 kcal, 3.5 g protein, 3.0 g fat (of which 0.5 g is saturated), 9.0 g carbohydrate, 4.0 g sugar, 3.0 g dietary fibre, and 450 mg sodium. Cholesterol is zero and trans fat is zero, consistent with its entirely plant-based composition. The dish is suitable for vegetarian and vegan diets. The high sodium content from soy sauce is the primary clinical concern, making it borderline for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management unless prepared with low-sodium soy sauce. For Type 2 Diabetes, the low carbohydrate content and meaningful dietary fibre make this a low-glycaemic choice with an estimated glycaemic index of approximately 20. The dish is a common ramen accompaniment in Singapore's Japanese restaurant scene.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for braised bamboo shoots with spring onions exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked bamboo shoots and scallions with adjustments for soy sauce sodium and sesame oil fat, consistent with the input record values. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature. Medium confidence is appropriate for a composite dish estimated from component ingredients.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile for this braised bamboo shoot and spring onion dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source as of 2026-04-16; the sesame oil component contributes primarily unsaturated fats but exact breakdown cannot be confirmed without laboratory analysis. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish preparation is not reported in available approved sources, though bamboo shoots are known to contain meaningful potassium (~533 mg/100 g raw per USDA); a cooked dish value cannot be reliably derived without confirmed recipe proportions. 3. water_g and remaining micronutrients (vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1 through vitamin_b12, folate, vitamin_d, vitamin_e, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium) are set to -1: these values are not available for this specific composite dish preparation in any approved source. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero as all ingredients are plant-based and no hydrogenated fats are used. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero as this is an entirely plant-based dish. 6. The input record listed the source URL as https://foodo.sg/ under HPB Singapore branding, but no specific entry for this dish was found in HPB Foodo; the URL was not used as a nutrient evidence source. USDA FoodData Central component estimates were used instead. 7. Halal status is marked false due to the presence of mirin (rice wine with alcohol content); the dish may not be suitable for halal dietary requirements without substitution of mirin with a non-alcoholic alternative. 8. GI of 20 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database.
